rationale: The citing paper references Lee (2002) multiple times to establish core premises: conceptual and perceptual fluency are distinct, and conceptual fluency has been linked to consideration-set membership and brand choice. It explicitly positions its contribution against that prior stream by arguing that work such as Lee (2002) focused on choice outcomes, whereas this paper tests effects on affective judgment. The paper also uses Lee (2002) to interpret a weak perceptual-fluency result in Experiment 1 (surface-feature overlap) and to motivate later design choices, indicating a meaningful role beyond a passing mention.
relation_description: The citing paper builds directly on Lee (2002)'s fluency framework and findings about choice-related consequences of implicit memory. It extends that line by examining how conceptual (and perceptual) fluency affects brand evaluation and by testing boundary conditions where conceptual fluency can become negatively valenced.
